coredata.dc:title: Advancements in S-scheme photocatalytic material for wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Manna, Soumyajit
Remya, Neelancherry
Singhal, Naresh
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, S-scheme photocatalytic treatment was used for removal of several pollutants from different industries to overcome the disadvantages of conventional photocatalytic treatment. This review provides an overview and comparison of degradation of various target pollutants like tetracycline (TC), ofloxacin (OFL), rhodamine B (RhB) and phenol using numerous S-scheme photocatalysts. Catalyst BiOBr (2.83 eV)/BiOAc1 −xBrx (3.28 eV) showed 99.2% removal efficiency for TC with 120 min when compared with other S-scheme photocatalyst. Higher catalyst dosage resulted in improved degradation rate of pollutant. For OFL, catalyst BiVO4 /CQDs/β-FeOOH exhibited highest efficiency of 99.21% with the lowest treatment time of 15 min which could be attributed due to peculiar molecular structure of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) plate. Catalyst g-C3N4/Bi/BiVO4 achieved 100% removal efficiency for RhB within 70 min treatment time. Similarly for phenol, catalyst Bi7O9I3 /Cd0.5 Zn0.5S QDs/WO3 −x (BCW) achieved 100% removal efficiency within 60 min treatment time. The mechanism of degradation of pollutants can be communicated depending upon the position and type of reactive sites. Pollutants are degraded to various intermediates and subsequent mineralization by mechanisms like cleavage, dealkylation, etc. In addition to various operating conditions that affect the S-scheme photocatalysis like catalyst dosage, treatment duration, pollutant concentration, degradation pathway of various pollutants and the cost assessment of the S-scheme photocatalyst are discussed in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S-scheme photocatalyst
Electrical energy per order
Pollutants degradation
Degradation pathway

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Engineering insight to design principle of S-scheme photocatalysts
Comparison of different S-scheme of photocatalysts for pollutants degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Energy band positions for different catalysts [3,5,9,16−32].

Removal efficiency of CIP vs time for different catalyst.
Removal efficiency of RhB vs time for different catalyst.
Removal efficiency of phenol vs time for different catalyst.

(a): Degradation pathway of Tetracycline by WO3/g-C3N4. Fig. 10(b): Degradation pathway of OFL using MWCNTs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst. Fig. 10 (c): Degradation pathway of CIP using SiC/g-C3N4 photocatalyst.

*All the values are either obtained or calculated from given data.
#All the values are either obtained or calculated from given data.

head.ce:title: Advancements in S-scheme photocatalytic material for wastewater treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Infrastructure, Indian Institute of Technology Bhubaneswar, Argul, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Infrastructure, Indian Institute of Technology Bhubaneswar

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Infrastructure, Indian Institute of Technology Bhubaneswar, Argul, Odisha India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, The University of Auckland, New Zealand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, The University of Auckland

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, The University of Auckland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, S-scheme photocatalytic treatment was used for removal of several pollutants from different industries to overcome the disadvantages of conventional photocatalytic treatment. This review provides an overview and comparison of degradation of various target pollutants like tetracycline (TC), ofloxacin (OFL), rhodamine B (RhB) and phenol using numerous S-scheme photocatalysts. Catalyst BiOBr (2.83 eV)/BiOAc1 −xBrx (3.28 eV) showed 99.2% removal efficiency for TC with 120 min when compared with other S-scheme photocatalyst. Higher catalyst dosage resulted in improved degradation rate of pollutant. For OFL, catalyst BiVO4 /CQDs/β-FeOOH exhibited highest efficiency of 99.21% with the lowest treatment time of 15 min which could be attributed due to peculiar molecular structure of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) plate. Catalyst g-C3N4/Bi/BiVO4 achieved 100% removal efficiency for RhB within 70 min treatment time. Similarly for phenol, catalyst Bi7O9I3 /Cd0.5 Zn0.5S QDs/WO3 −x (BCW) achieved 100% removal efficiency within 60 min treatment time. The mechanism of degradation of pollutants can be communicated depending upon the position and type of reactive sites. Pollutants are degraded to various intermediates and subsequent mineralization by mechanisms like cleavage, dealkylation, etc. In addition to various operating conditions that affect the S-scheme photocatalysis like catalyst dosage, treatment duration, pollutant concentration, degradation pathway of various pollutants and the cost assessment of the S-scheme photocatalyst are discussed in detail.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The drastic increment of the global population increased the demand for natural resources like energy, water, etc. With the development of mankind, different products from diverse industries like healthcare, beverages, heavy industries, etc. are being added to our day-to-day life. Increment of consumption of these products resulted in increased waste generation in the form of different toxic compounds like phenols, pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCP), dyes, other organic contaminants as well as pathogens that expose human health to higher risk [1–5]. Traditional technologies like heating, chemical – halogenation, ozonation and UV irradiation poses different drawbacks like high energy consumption, formation of chemical residues, and recolonization of pathogens which necessitates the development of an efficient and economic method for the removal of these toxic compounds [6–8].

Photocatalysis using semiconductor photocatalysts is one of the most convenient ways to degrade these pollutants from wastewater. TiO2 is highly adopted in the commercial market for the photocatalytic degradation of heavy metals, organic pollutants and PPCP due to its desirable corrosion resistance, high light-conversion efficiency, nontoxicity, durability, and low cost [9]. In addition to TiO2, several studies focused on the development of other metals as well as non-metal based photocatalyst (Eg: Bi, B (Borates) [10], Ce [11], C, ZnO, MoS2 [6] etc.). However, these photocatalysts have several drawbacks like poor energy utilization due to higher bandgap, rapid recombination of electron (e-) and hole (h+) pair, high cost, and pollutions by leaching. Therefore, a rigorous effort is put by several researchers to overcome these issues. One such method is doping/coupling of two metal compounds to enhance the excitation wavelength and reduce e--h+ recombination.

In the early stage of the development of coupled photocatalysts, the type-II charge transfer mechanism (type – II heterojunction) was introduced. In this case, the generation of pairs of holes and electrons from the semiconductor-I (S1) and semiconductor-II (S2) respectively ( Fig. 1(a)). Photogenerated holes traverse from S2 to S1 and in case of electrons, it is being happened conversely. Drawbacks like thermodynamic instability due to electron-electron and hole-hole repulsion and the difference between theoretical and real pathway of electron conduction come into picture. Similarly, the introduction of carriers of electrons in the Z-scheme (Fig. 1(b)) imposes high level of inefficiency of this mechanism [12].

The new step-scheme (S-scheme) mechanism had been introduced to overcome thermodynamic inefficiency and electron transfer mechanism. An S-Scheme photocatalyst consists of one reduction photocatalyst (RP) and another oxidation photocatalyst (OP) [13]. A higher conduction band of RP provides a better reduction potential whereas lower valence band of OP provides efficient oxidation[12] ( Fig. 2). RP semiconductor possess higher fermi level (Ef) and CB compared to OP semiconductor. In the coupled system, it is generally observed that the Ef of RP moves downward and Ef of OP moves upwards until equalisation of the both fermi level. The Ef at the contact point of OP and RP is equal and a gradient is established in the other regions which results a bend in fermi level. The reason behind this is the flow of electron from RP semiconductor to OP semiconductor leaving the positive charges in RP semiconductor due to higher fermi level [14]. The increase in the electron density in the interface of OP semiconductor and decrease in RP semiconductor results in the rise and drop of Ef, consecutively [15]. Thus the S-scheme photocatalyst shows optimum redox capability [13]. Different junctions with both n-type and p-type semiconductor provide different types of junctions namely n-n, n-p, p-n, p-p.

S-scheme photocatalyst is being developed using different semiconductors through variety of synthesis routes. Some semiconductors like TiO2 and BiOBr are being used as both OP and RP [9,12]. The degradation of various target pollutants like tetracycline (TC), ofloxacin (OFL), rhodamine B (RhB) and phenol using various S-scheme photocatalysts is compared and investigated in this review. In addition, the economic analysis – electrical energy consumption and degradation mechanism also been compared in this review.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engineering insight to design principle of S-scheme photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Band positions for different semiconductor materials that have been used for the different modifications are shown in Fig. 3. The position of bands (VB and CB) and the bandgaps (Eg) play a major role in the development of new S-scheme photocatalysts. The OP semiconductor and the RP semiconductor selected respectively for the oxidative and reductive removal of pollutants. The photon capable of exciting the electron from VB to CB is obtained from the relation bandgap (Eg) is 1240/ λ where λ is wavelength of light (nm). Hence to make visible light (λ ∈(400 nm,700 nm)) excited photocatalyst, the photocatalyst requires the Eg between 1.77 eV and 3.1 eV. The semiconductor photocatalyst with Eg beyond 1.77 eV and 3.1 eV can be converted to visible light excited photocatalyst by combining the suitable OP with RP to form staggered band structures. An S-scheme heterojunction is a visible light excited catalyst which introduces a strong redox potential by reserving the electrons and holes respectively in the CB of RP and VB of OP and combining the photogenerated charge carriers, thus resembling a “step like” charge transfer route.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different S-scheme of photocatalysts for pollutants degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Carcinogenicity of tetracycline (TC) and RhB becomes a high alert for human health and environment. Different modifications had been done on different pharmaceutical pollutants (tetracycline (TC), ofloxacin (OFL), dyes (rhodamine B (RhB)) as well as very common pollutants like CO2 and phenols. But different catalysts achieved different efficiency levels for pollutant removal as well as different types of performance in terms of recyclability.
Fig. 4 compares the TC removal efficiency using various S-scheme catalyst along with the treatment time. Operating condition and degradation rate in various treatment system is shown in Table 1.

TC removal efficiency using various catalyst ranged from 80% to 99.2%. BiOBr (2.83 eV)/BiOAc1 −xBrx (3.28 eV) showed a better efficiency (99.2%) for removal of TC compared to other S-scheme photocatalysts within 120 min with the catalyst dosage of 0.4 g/L. This could be due to the dispersed flower-like morphology and improved surface area of BiOBr/BiOAc1 −xBrx ( Fig. 5) which leads to more electron carrying capacity that regards the efficiency in visible light. Conversely, 95% efficiency was achieved within 50 min using WO3/CdIn2S4 with a catalyst dosage of 1 g/L. The higher catalyst dosage (2.5 times) resulted in the improved degradation rate of WO3/CdIn2S4 system, which is nearly 4.5 times of BiOBr/BiOAc1 −xBrx system and the respective values are 0.0599 min−1 and 0.0134 min−1 (Fig. 4 and Table 1).

The OFL removal efficiency using various S-scheme catalysts along with the treatment time is illustrated in Fig. 6. Application of catalysts in different operating condition and degradation rate in various treatment systems is depicted in in Table 2.

OFL removal efficiencies varied from 79.00% to 99.21%. BCF with CQDs: BiVO4 of 1:10 showed the best removal efficiency of 99.21% with a lowest time of 15 min at a catalyst dosage 0.6 g/L and OFL concentration of 10 mg/L with the highest rate constant of 0.2500 min−1 (Fig. 6 & Table 2). This could be attributed to peculiar molecular structure of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and plate-like morphology of BiVO4 which resulted in the improved surface area and increase the electron transfer. The adsorption and activation behaviour had been optimized by the surface oxygen vacancy. The variation of CQDs in BiVO4 from 1:30–1:6 provided negligible changes in term of efficiencies (more than 98% removal efficiency within 20 min).
Fig. 7 compares the CIP removal efficiency using various S-scheme catalyst along with the treatment time. Operating condition and degradation rate in various treatment system is shown in Table 3.

CIP removal efficiency using different catalysts ranged from 95% to 72%. The SiC/g-C3N4 showed the best removal efficiency of 95% within 30 min at a catalyst dosage 0.4 g/L and CIP concentration 10 mg/L with a highest rate constant 0.132 min−1. Dispersed g-C3N4 on the SiC effectively increased the surface area of the catalyst which helps in the higher photocatalytic activity.

RhB removal efficiency using different S-Scheme catalysts along with the treatment time is shown in Fig. 8. The application of catalysts under different operating conditions and degradation rates in different treatment systems are described in Table 4.

RhB removal efficiencies varied from 82% to 100%. Catalyst g-C3N4/Bi/BiVO4 achieved 100% efficiency with the treatment duration 70 min whereas 99% efficiency is achieved with 40 min duration by MoS2/Ag3PO4 under the same pollutant concentration of 10 mg/L with catalyst dosage 1 g/L and 0.25 g/L consecutively (Fig. 8 & Table 4). The rate constant observed for MoS2/Ag3PO4 was 0.1108 min−1 which is 1.7 times more than g-C3N4/Bi/BiVO4 (0.067 min−1).

Fig. 9 compares the TC removal efficiency using various S-scheme catalyst along with the treatment time. Operating condition and degradation rate in various treatment system is shown in Table 5. ( Table 6).

Catalyst Bi7O9I3/Cd0.5Zn0.5S QDs/WO3 −x (BCW) achieved best phenol removal efficiency of 100% with the least treatment time of 60 min at a catalyst dosage 0.5 g/L and phenol concentration 10 mg/L with highest rate constant of 0.0259 min−1 (Fig. 7 and Table 4).

Pure Bi7O9I3 illustrated a 3D flower-shaped microsphere which was formed from evenly distributed many tiny and wrinkled nano-sheets with thickness of each 20 µm. On the other hand, irregular and fine nanosheets of Cd0.5Zn0.5S QDs exhibited rough and porous surfaces, and WO3−x samples demonstrated variable size clusters consisted of a large number of irregular and flat nanowires [8,25]. There for the combination of Bi7O9I3, Cd0.5Zn0.5S QDs and WO3 −x resulted improved surface-specific area, phenol adsorption improvement and subsequent photocatalytic degradation.

Electrical energy efficiency measurement had been considered as a new parameter that led to economically choosing catalyst usage in large scales industries like brewery, pharmaceutical, textile, dye, soft drink industries etc. The standardization of the term electrical energy per order by Bolton et al. [40] was approved by IUPAC [41]. Electrical energy per order (EEO) is the number of kWh of electrical energy needed to eliminate the pollutant in 1 m3 of contaminated water by one order of magnitude [41]. The EEO (kWh/L) is calculated as per Eq. (1).
(1)
EEO =
P
60 * v *
k
overall
Where, P is the power of lamp (kW), V is the working volume (L), and koverall is observed overall rate constant (min−1). The cost of treatment using the respective catalyst was calculated as per Eq. (2).
(2)
Cost =
EEO
c
0
−
c
f
* Unit Price
Where, Cost is in INR, Unit Price is in INR/kWh, EEO in kWh and
c
0
,
c
f
is initial and final concentration of pollutants (mg/L) respectively.

The commercial tariff is India for per unit electricity (1kWh) is 7.75 INR is adopted for calculating the cost of treatment. For TC degradation, WO3/g-C3N4 exhibits least EEO of 2.646 kWh L−1 and costing 1.1322 INR/mg. Similarly, BCF, Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4 and BCW are the most cost effective for OFL, RhB and phenol treatment with per mg costing 0.0521, 0.9827, 0.0015 INR respectively (Table 5). The catalyst BiOBr/BiO (HCOO)Br-x had EEO of 12.424 kWh L−1 and 6.944 kWh L−1 for TC and RhB treatment costing 6.0181 INR and 2.7459 INR respectively.

Photocatalysts with rich oxygen vacancies tended to activate O2 molecules into reactive oxygen species (ROS, i.e., ⋅
O
2
−
, H2O2, and ⋅OH) to efficiently oxidize organic pollutants [42,43]. Pollutants have different active sites where the ROS could attack and starts the reaction. Depending upon the position and type of reactive sites, pollutants are degraded to various intermediates and subsequent mineralization to carbon dioxide and water by mechanisms like cleavage, dealkylation, etc. The intermediates are analysed using GC/MS and LC/MS to identify the degradation mechanism. Based on the end products (aromatic and non-aromatic) with different m/z ratios, the proposed degradation pathway of TC with WO3/g-C3N4 as catalyst is presented in Fig. 10(a) [31].

The pathway B started with demethylation from the amino group which resulted a product with m/z value 431. In the pathways A and C, the products with m/z of 301 and 165 were generated from TC by losing of amino group and breaking the cyclic hydrocarbon structure respectively. End products through the pathway from B3 and B4 of m/z 150 and 118 generated with the clevage of ring and and deamylation and similarly an intermediate product with m/z of 341 was produced which went further ring clevage and removal of ketogroup to form a product with m/z of 223. A compound of m/z of 279 had been formed through pathway B1 after the removal of a complete ring from its parent compound and through pathway A after removal of keto group from the respective parent compound. The obtainted product with m/z of 279, produced two by-products through the mechanism of ring clevage with m/z of 137 and 149 which is also produced from the intermediate product from B2 through the same mechanism. The product with m/z of 223 went through two steps of removal of alchoholic group and methyl group to reach m/z of 137 which further went to deoxylation to produced another final product with m/z 133. Finally all end products mineralised into CO2 and H2O.

Photocatalytic treatment of OFL using MWCNTs/Bi5O7I catalyst exhibited TOC removal efficiency of 47.5%. The four possible pathway of OFL degradation using MWCNTs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 8(b) [32]. The pathway A is initiated through dealkylation and oxidation of piperazine ring followed by decarboxylation. Similarly, B initiated through oxidation and cleavage of piperazine ring followed by decarboxylation and demethylation. Pathway C was started with the dealkylation reaction of piperazine ring. All the pathways (A to D) resulted in the formation of acetic acid which went on further decomposition to provide CO2 and H2O.

The degradation of CIP using SiC/g-C
3
N
4 is started with two pathways which is shown in Fig. 10(a). In the pathway A, the compound with m/z value 362 is produced with the opening of the piperzine group and it produced the following compound with m/z value 334 with the process of decarbonylation. Similarly, the compound with m/z value 306 was produced from furthur decarbonylation from the previous compound. With furthur demanination and decarbonylation, the compound with m/z 291 is produced. Furthur decarboxylation leads to produce end product of A1 pathway a compound with m/z 246. The pathway A2 started due to decarbonylation of compound with m/z 291 to derive a product with m/z value 263. The following compound with deflourination generated an new compound with m/z value 245. The oxidation of the amino group of the previous compound to produce the final product of A2 pathway with m/z value 274.

In the pathway B, the new compound with m/z value 346 was formed through defluorination and hydroxylation of CIP and furthur compound with m/z 301 is formed through decarboxylation and this becomes the final product of this pathway.

The recent research advancements in the treatment of TC, OFL, RhB and phenol using S-scheme photocatalyst have been studied giving emphasis to the optimization of the process in terms of removal efficiency and cost. It is evident from the studies that the type of catalyst, operating condition and versatility played an important role in the pollutant removal. For high efficiency multiple pollutants treatment, usage of single catalyst like BiOBr/BiO(HCOO)Br-x (80% for TC, 98% for RhB), BiOBr/BiOAc1 −xBrx (99.2% for TC, 99.4% for RhB) etc. has also been studied. Optimisation of removal efficiency and cost is considered for maximum pollutant degradation. BiOBr (2.83 eV)/BiOAc1 −xBrx (3.28 eV) showed a better efficiency (99.2%) for removal of TC compared to other S-scheme photocatalysts within 120 min but least treatment time is required for TC removal for WO3/CdIn2S4 with 2.5 times catalyst dosage of the previous one. For OFL degradation, BCF is an optimized solution with 100% removal efficiency with least treatment time i.e., 15 min. For RhB degradation catalyst g-C3N4/Bi/BiVO4 showed 100% removal efficiency in 70 min whereas catalyst MoS2/Ag3PO4 was economical with a removal efficiency of 98% in 40 min. For phenol degradation, catalyst BCW had removal efficiency of 100% in 60 min. The degradation pathways for pollutant TC resulted in different end products which mineralised into CO2 and H2O and in case of OFL, it is resulted in the formation of acetic acid which went on further decomposed to CO2 and H2O. Least EEO is found for TC is 7.246 kWhL−1 with the catalyst BiOBr/BiOAc1 −xBrx, for OFL 0.067 kWhL−1 with catalyst BCF, for RhB 0.619 kWhL-1 Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4 and for Phenol 0.002 kWhL−1 with catalyst BCW. Similarly, it is also observable that least cost also generated per mg removal in case of these sets of catalysts and pollutants respectively (INR 2.8249, 0.0521, 0.9827 and 0.0015). The overview of this discussion will provide a new scope to the pathway of development of new single photocatalytic materials with increased efficiency and low cost for multiple industry pollutants.
Not applicable.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
